Name: simpsons-paradox
Description: Digital mirror of the Simpson's Paradox domain. The entities are Studies, Treatments, Strata, and CaseCells (raw patient counts). SuccessRates, TreatmentSummaries, and TreatmentRankings are derived. The paradox falls out of the DAG as an emergent derived fact — it is never modeled directly.

Loops.Description: Table: Loops — the 10-loop build plan, carried as a first-class entity inside the model. Each row documents what new concept is introduced and why.

schema.name: LoopId
schema.Description: Unique identifier: loop-01 through loop-10.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Short title for what this loop adds to the domain model.

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: planned | in-progress | complete

schema.name: NewConcept
schema.Description: The new first-class domain concept introduced this loop.

schema.name: DomainQuestion
schema.Description: The natural-language question this loop's concept answers FROM the domain data.

schema.name: MockDataNote
schema.Description: What the seed data is designed to show.

schema.name: NextSuggestion
schema.Description: Suggestion for the next loop, written after witnessing the DAG at this loop's end.

data.Title: Core domain: Study, Treatment, Stratum, CaseCell → SuccessRate falls out
data.NewConcept: Studies, Treatments, Strata, CaseCells
data.DomainQuestion: For each (stratum, treatment) cell in a study, what is the success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Kidney stone study (Charig 1986): 2 strata (small/large), 2 treatments (A/B), 4 cells. CellSuccessRate is the first derived fact.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each treatment, what is its pooled (across all strata) success rate? That requires TreatmentSummary — aggregating CaseCells by treatment, ignoring stratum.

data.Title: TreatmentSummary: pooled rate per treatment across all strata
data.NewConcept: TreatmentSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: If you ignore stratum entirely, what is each treatment's overall success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Pooled: A=273/350=78%, B=289/350=83%. B looks better — this is the misleading aggregate view.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each (stratum, treatment) pair, what is the success rate WITHIN that stratum? That requires StratumSummary — the view that contradicts the pooled view.

data.Title: StratumSummary: per-stratum rate per treatment — the true picture
data.NewConcept: StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: Within each stratum, which treatment has the higher success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Small: A=93.1% > B=86.7%. Large: A=73.0% > B=68.8%. A wins in BOTH strata. Contradiction with pooled view is now visible.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each treatment pair in a study, does the pooled winner match the per-stratum winner? That requires TreatmentRanking — the node where the paradox either exists or doesn't.

data.Title: TreatmentRanking: does the pooled winner = the per-stratum winner?
data.NewConcept: TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: When pooled and stratified conclusions disagree — which treatment should actually be preferred?
data.MockDataNote: Pooled winner = B. Per-stratum winner = A in every stratum. Simpson's Paradox: IsReversal = true. This is the paradox as a derived fact, not a modeled entity.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: WHY does the paradox occur? The stratifying variable (stone size) affects both treatment assignment AND outcome. Name it as a StratumVariable with a CausalRole field.

data.Title: StratumVariable: name the confounding factor, its causal role, and derive IsConfounder + IsParadoxExplained
data.NewConcept: StratumVariables (one per study); CausalRole field; IsConfounder boolean; IsParadoxExplained on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: What IS the stratum variable, why does it confound, and does the model witness its own explanation?
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: VariableName=stone_size, CausalRole=confounder, AffectsTreatmentAssignment=true, AffectsOutcome=true → IsConfounder=true → IsParadoxExplained=true on TreatmentRankings.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: how large is the allocation imbalance? Derive TreatmentExposureFraction on CaseCells — the fraction of each treatment's cases that land in each stratum. High imbalance = confounding mechanism as a witnessed number.

data.Title: TreatmentExposureFraction: the allocation imbalance as a witnessed number
data.NewConcept: TreatmentExposureFraction field on CaseCells (Cases / TotalCasesForTreatment in this study)
data.DomainQuestion: What fraction of each treatment's total cases fall in each stratum? High imbalance across strata is the mechanism of confounding.
data.MockDataNote: A: 263/350=75% in large-stone stratum. B: 80/350=23% in large-stone stratum. The 52-point gap IS the mechanism — now a first-class derived number.
data.NextSuggestion: Add a degenerate study where treatment allocation is perfectly balanced across strata. IsReversal must be FALSE — not because the data says so, but because the algebra guarantees it. The model witnesses its own structural impossibility.

data.Title: Degenerate study: balanced allocation → IsReversal structurally impossible
data.NewConcept: Second Studies row (balanced-synthetic); equal TreatmentExposureFraction across strata; IsReversal = FALSE by construction
data.DomainQuestion: If every stratum gets the same treatment-allocation fraction, can a reversal occur? The DAG should witness that it cannot.
data.MockDataNote: Synthetic: A and B each assigned 50/50 to small/large strata. A=60% success both strata, B=50% both. Pooled: A=60%, B=50%. No reversal. IsParadoxExplained vacuously true (IsConfounder=false, no confounding, no reversal).
data.NextSuggestion: Add Berkeley 1973 — same DAG, contested domain. Aggregate: women admitted at lower rate. Per-department: women admitted at equal or higher rate in most. IsReversal=true. But IsConfounder is ambiguous — department choice may be downstream of gender (mediator, not confounder). The model witnesses its own explanatory limits.

data.Title: Berkeley 1973: contested confounder — model reveals a PARTIAL paradox, not a clean reversal
data.NewConcept: Third Studies row (berkeley-1973); StratumVariable=department; CausalRole=contested; IsConfounder=false; IsParadoxExplained=false; PerStratumWinner=none (mixed, not unanimous)
data.DomainQuestion: Does Berkeley show a clean reversal? No — PerStratumWinner='none' because the per-stratum winner is mixed (female wins 3/4 depts, male wins 1). The model surfaces a structural limit of the reversal definition: partial paradoxes are real but don't satisfy the strict IsReversal criterion.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed: male pooled 52.8% vs female 39.9% (large gap). Per dept: female wins A (82%>62%), B (68%>63%), D (35%>33%); male wins C (37%>34%). No unanimous per-stratum winner → IsReversal=FALSE by strict definition. The intuitive paradox is real; the formal paradox is partial.
data.NextSuggestion: Add ParadoxStrength (scalar severity) to capture partial reversals — pooled gap and average per-stratum gap regardless of unanimity. Then StudySummary for epistemic coverage across all 3 studies.

data.Title: ParadoxStrength: scalar severity derived from pooled gap and per-stratum gap
data.NewConcept: ParadoxStrength on TreatmentRankings; PooledGap and AvgStratumGap on StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: How large is the reversal? Can we rank studies by paradox severity, and does strength correlate with allocation imbalance?
data.MockDataNote: Kidney: pooled gap ~4.6pp favoring B, avg stratum gap ~5pp favoring A. Berkeley: larger pooled gap, varies by department. Balanced: strength=0 (no reversal).
data.NextSuggestion: StudySummary: roll up ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, AvgParadoxStrength across all studies. The model now describes its own epistemic coverage in a single view.

data.Title: StudySummary: the model witnesses its own epistemic coverage
data.NewConcept: StudySummary table (one row per model instance): StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, UnexplainedCount, ImpossibleCount, AvgParadoxStrength
data.DomainQuestion: Across all studies in this model, what is the epistemic state? How many reversals, how many explained, how many witnessed-but-unexplained?
data.MockDataNote: 3 studies: ReversalCount=2 (kidney+berkeley), ExplainedCount=1 (kidney), UnexplainedCount=1 (berkeley), ImpossibleCount=1 (balanced). The model now holds a mirror to itself.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: how heavy is each stratum? Add StratumFraction to StratumSummaries — the fraction of the study's total cases that land in each stratum. The weight each stratum carries IS the lever the confounder pulls.

data.Title: StratumFraction: the weight each stratum carries in the pooled average
data.NewConcept: StratumFraction (and StudyTotalCases, StratumTotalCases) on StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: What fraction of the study's total cases land in each stratum? High StratumFraction on a low-success stratum pulls the pooled rate down — and when that stratum is also over-allocated to one treatment, the reversal follows.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: large-stratum = (263+80)/700 = 49%; small-stratum = (87+270)/700 = 51%. Nearly equal stratum weights — but unequal per-treatment allocation (75% of A is in large; 23% of B is in large). The weight alone is not the mechanism; the differential allocation into heavy strata is.
data.NextSuggestion: Add WeightedStratumRate: StratumFraction × StratumSuccessRate per treatment row. Sum these across strata to reconstruct PooledRate from first principles. The DAG now witnesses the mechanism equation: pooled rate = weighted average of stratum rates.

data.Title: WeightedPull: mechanism equation witnessed — pooled rate = weighted sum of stratum rates
data.NewConcept: WeightedStratumRate on StratumSummaries; PooledRateFromWeightsA and PooledRateFromWeightsB on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: Can the DAG reconstruct each treatment's pooled rate purely from stratum weights × stratum rates? If PooledRateFromWeights = PooledRate, the mechanism equation is verified: Simpson's Paradox is algebraically explained.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: PooledRateFromWeightsA=0.833, PooledRateFromWeightsB=0.779 (A looks better under equal study-level weights). But actual pooled rates are A=0.780, B=0.826 (B looks better). The divergence IS the mechanism: real pooled uses treatment-level allocation weights; the reconstruction uses study-level weights. The swap between the two orderings shows exactly how differential allocation flips the conclusion. balanced-synthetic: weights match exactly (equal allocation = no flip possible).
data.NextSuggestion: Add AllocationBias to StratumSummaries: the difference between TreatmentExposureFraction for A and B in each stratum. High bias toward the losing treatment in a low-success stratum IS the confounder's fingerprint as a witnessed number.

data.Title: AllocationBias: the confounder's fingerprint as a signed witnessed number
data.NewConcept: AllocationBias on StratumSummaries (AllocationFractionA - AllocationFractionB); also AllocationFractionA, AllocationFractionB, TreatmentACasesHere, TreatmentBCasesHere, TreatmentATotalCases, TreatmentBTotalCases
data.DomainQuestion: In each stratum, is one treatment disproportionately concentrated here? AllocationBias = AllocationFractionA - AllocationFractionB: positive means A is over-represented here, negative means B is. A strong negative bias in a low-success stratum is the confounder's fingerprint.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986 large-stratum: AllocationFractionA=263/350=0.751, AllocationFractionB=80/350=0.229, AllocationBias=+0.523. A is massively over-allocated to the hard (large-stone) stratum. This single number summarizes why the pooled rate misleads: A carries 52pp more exposure to the hard case, dragging its pooled average down below B's despite beating B in both strata.
data.NextSuggestion: Add a 4th study: kidney-balanced — same stratum rates as kidney-1986 but with equal allocation (50/50 split). AllocationBias=0 everywhere, IsReversal=FALSE. The counterfactual shows the reversal is purely an allocation artifact, not a property of the treatment rates themselves.

data.Title: Counterfactual: kidney-balanced isolates allocation as the sole cause of the reversal
data.NewConcept: Fourth study (kidney-balanced): same stratum rates as kidney-1986, equal allocation (175 cases per cell). IsReversal=FALSE, AllocationBias=0, ParadoxStrength=0.
data.DomainQuestion: If the same doctors, same disease, same treatments, same stratum difficulty — but patients were allocated equally — would the paradox still exist? The DAG answers: no. The reversal lives in the allocation, not in the treatment rates.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-balanced: PooledRateA = (163+128)/350 = 0.831, PooledRateB = (152+120)/350 = 0.777. A wins pooled AND per-stratum. IsReversal=FALSE, ParadoxStrength=0. AllocationBias=0 in both strata. The counterfactual witnesses that the reversal in kidney-1986 is entirely an artifact of differential allocation, not of treatment efficacy.
data.NextSuggestion: Add ReversalThreshold to TreatmentRankings: the minimum AllocationBias magnitude needed to flip the pooled winner. How far is kidney-1986 from the threshold? How close is berkeley-1973? This quantifies paradox fragility vs robustness.

data.Title: ReversalThreshold: how robust or fragile is each study's paradox?
data.NewConcept: MaxAllocationBias, WeightedStratumGapSum, ThresholdMargin on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: How much allocation bias is needed to produce a reversal? Is the observed bias well above threshold (robust paradox) or barely over it (fragile paradox)? ThresholdMargin = observed bias minus minimum needed.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: ReversalIntensity=1.0, ThresholdMargin=+0.5 — maximally robust, every stratum opposes pooled. berkeley-1973: ReversalIntensity=0.75, ThresholdMargin=+0.25 — past threshold but not unanimous (3/4 departments oppose). balanced-synthetic and kidney-balanced: intensity=0, margin=-0.5 — structurally impossible. ThresholdMargin > 0 means the paradox is robust; < 0 means absent or impossible.
data.NextSuggestion: Pour a real-world dataset with a known mediator (not confounder) through the same DAG and witness where it lands on the reversal_intensity × paradox_strength plane. The geometry of the plane is the next frontier.

data.Title: StratumGap: per-stratum rate difference as a signed witnessed number
data.NewConcept: StratumGap field on StratumSummaries (StratumRateA - StratumRateB, signed)
data.DomainQuestion: In each stratum, how large is the treatment advantage, and in which direction? StratumGap > 0 means A wins here; < 0 means B wins. Summing StratumGap × StratumFraction gives the equal-weight pooled gap — a new derived node.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: small-stratum gap = +0.064 (A wins by 6.4pp), large-stratum gap = +0.043 (A wins by 4.3pp). Both positive — A wins everywhere. The pooled gap is negative (B appears to win by 4.6pp) purely due to allocation. The signed gap makes this contradiction arithmetically visible in one column. kidney-balanced: small=+0.064, large=+0.042 (same signs, same magnitudes — the rates are identical to kidney-1986 but now A also wins pooled because allocation is balanced). balanced-synthetic: small=+0.10, large=+0.10 (constant, equal allocation). berkeley-1973: dept-A=−0.200 (female wins), dept-B=+0.011 (male wins barely), dept-C=−0.002 (female wins barely), dept-D=−0.016 (female wins). Mixed signs confirm the partial paradox: no unanimous StratumGap direction, hence no clean reversal.
data.NextSuggestion: Derive WeightedStratumGapSum on TreatmentRankings: SUM(StratumGap × StratumFraction) aggregated across all TreatmentA rows for this study. This is the equal-weight pooled gap — the gap allocation-free weighting would produce. When WeightedStratumGapSum and PooledGap have opposite signs, the allocation has not just compressed the signal but flipped it. That sign flip is a more precise characterisation of Simpson's Paradox than IsReversal: it handles partial flips and gives a magnitude to the distortion.

data.Title: WeightedStratumGapSum: the equal-weight signal — and the Berkeley category error witnessed
data.NewConcept: WeightedStratumGap on StratumSummaries (StratumGap × StratumFraction, the per-stratum contribution to the equal-weight pooled gap); WeightedStratumGapSum on TreatmentRankings (SUMIFS of WeightedStratumGap across TreatmentA rows); SignedPooledGap (PooledRateA − PooledRateB, signed); IsSignFlip boolean (WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs)
data.DomainQuestion: If allocation were equal across strata, what would the pooled gap be? WeightedStratumGapSum answers this. When it has the opposite sign to SignedPooledGap, the allocation has not just compressed the signal — it has flipped it. That sign flip IS Simpson's Paradox under a continuous definition. Does Berkeley sign-flip?
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB: balanced-synthetic: WSGSUM=+0.1000, SignedPooledGap=+0.1000 → IsSignFlip=FALSE (equal allocation, no distortion). kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457 → IsSignFlip=TRUE (A wins in every stratum by 5.4pp on average, but actual pooled shows B winning by 4.6pp). kidney-balanced: WSGSUM=+0.0543, SignedPooledGap=+0.0543 → IsSignFlip=FALSE (equal allocation, WSGSUM = actual pooled gap). berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292 → IsSignFlip=TRUE. Per-stratum breakdown: dept-A gap=−0.2035 (female wins by 20.4pp, fraction=0.289); dept-B=−0.0496 (female wins, fraction=0.181); dept-C=+0.0286 (male wins barely, fraction=0.284); dept-D=−0.0184 (female wins, fraction=0.245). Under equal allocation female would win pooled by 6.4pp — but the actual pooled shows male winning by 12.9pp. Berkeley IS a Simpson's Paradox under the sign-flip definition despite IsReversal=FALSE. The binary unanimity criterion excluded Berkeley because dept-C goes to male; the sign-flip criterion correctly includes it.
data.NextSuggestion: The sign flip is witnessed. Now measure the magnitude: how far has the allocation bent the pooled signal? AllocationDistortion = |WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|. Berkeley: |−0.0642 − (+0.1292)| = 0.1934. Kidney: |+0.0537 − (−0.0457)| = 0.0994. Berkeley's distortion is nearly twice kidney's — despite IsReversal=FALSE. This is the single-number measure that ranks studies by how badly confounding has distorted their pooled result.

data.Title: AllocationDistortion: the magnitude of the signal the allocation erased or fabricated
data.NewConcept: AllocationDistortion on TreatmentRankings (|WeightedStratumGapSum − PooledGapSigned|); SignedPooledGap (PooledRateA − PooledRateB, signed not absolute)
data.DomainQuestion: How much has the allocation distorted the pooled signal relative to what equal-weight strata would show? AllocationDistortion=0 means the allocation is neutral (balanced-synthetic, kidney-balanced). A large AllocationDistortion means the allocation is doing most of the work — the pooled number is mostly noise from how cases were assigned, not signal about which treatment works.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: AllocationDistortion = |+0.054 − (−0.046)| = 0.100. The 10pp distortion completely reverses a +5.4pp true signal into an apparent −4.6pp. berkeley-1973: AllocationDistortion = |−0.064 − (+0.129)| = 0.193. The allocation is injecting a +12.9pp apparent male advantage on top of an underlying −6.4pp female advantage. Berkeley's distortion is nearly twice kidney's — the model now witnesses this unambiguously. balanced-synthetic and kidney-balanced: AllocationDistortion ≈ 0 (allocation is neutral).
data.NextSuggestion: The model now has two measures of paradox severity: ParadoxStrength (the old scalar: PooledGap × ReversalIntensity) and AllocationDistortion (the new one: how far the pooled signal has been bent). They rank the same studies differently. Ask which one is the better measure — and whether they can be combined into a single ordered score.

data.Title: DistortionRank: do ParadoxStrength and AllocationDistortion agree? Which measure is right?
data.NewConcept: DistortionRank on TreatmentRankings (ordinal rank by AllocationDistortion across studies in ModelSummary); comparison of ParadoxStrength rank vs AllocationDistortion rank per study
data.DomainQuestion: ParadoxStrength = PooledGap × ReversalIntensity. AllocationDistortion = |WSGSUM − PooledGapSigned|. Do they rank studies the same way? If they disagree, which ranking is more meaningful — and why? The answer determines which measure belongs in the instrument.
data.MockDataNote: By AllocationDistortion: berkeley(0.193) > kidney-1986(0.100) > kidney-balanced(≈0) = balanced-synthetic(≈0). By ParadoxStrength: berkeley(0.097) > kidney-1986(0.046) > kidney-balanced(0) = balanced-synthetic(0). The ordinal ranking is the same but the magnitudes tell different stories: ParadoxStrength suppresses Berkeley (because ReversalIntensity is 0.75 not 1.0), while AllocationDistortion elevates it. AllocationDistortion does not penalise Berkeley for lacking unanimity — it measures what the allocation DID to the signal, not whether the outcome crosses an arbitrary threshold.
data.NextSuggestion: AllocationDistortion is the cleaner measure because it is indifferent to the unanimity criterion. But it requires WSGSUM, which requires StratumGap and StratumFraction — two loops of upstream work. Ask: can AllocationDistortion be approximated from published summary statistics alone (no raw stratum data required)? That approximability determines whether this instrument can screen the literature.

data.Title: ScreeningApproximation: can AllocationDistortion be estimated from published summary statistics?
data.NewConcept: ScreeningApprox field on TreatmentRankings: an estimate of AllocationDistortion computable from pooled rates and stratum-level published summaries without access to raw CaseCells; ApproxError = |ScreeningApprox − AllocationDistortion|
data.DomainQuestion: Published studies routinely report pooled rates and sometimes per-stratum rates, but rarely individual-level data. Can the key plane coordinates be approximated from what is in a published table? If yes, the instrument can screen any published study. If no, it requires raw data access — which limits its reach.
data.MockDataNote: Approximation attempt: if a paper reports pooled rates for A and B and at least one stratum-level rate, WSGSUM can be bounded. Test on kidney-1986 and berkeley-1973 using only what Charig 1986 and Bickel 1975 actually published in their tables. Compute ApproxError. If ApproxError < 0.02 for both, the approximation is sufficient for screening.
data.NextSuggestion: If the screening approximation is valid, pour a third real-world published study through the pipeline using only published summary statistics (no raw data). This is the first external test of the instrument's reach. Choose a study from a domain neither kidney nor Berkeley covers.

data.Title: Third real dataset: a published study from a new domain poured through the DAG
data.NewConcept: Fifth study from a third domain (candidates: COVID-19 vaccine efficacy by age/comorbidity; death penalty sentencing by defendant/victim race; graduate school admissions by program type); AllocationDistortion and IsSignFlip witnessed from published data
data.DomainQuestion: Does the instrument's geometry generalise beyond the two canonical examples? A third domain study either (a) has IsSignFlip=TRUE and high AllocationDistortion — confirming the instrument identifies a new paradox — or (b) has IsSignFlip=FALSE — confirming the instrument correctly withholds the classification. Either result is informative.
data.MockDataNote: To be filled after dataset selection. Priority criteria: (1) published, peer-reviewed, with stratum-level counts accessible; (2) from a domain with consequential pooled conclusions (clinical, legal, or policy); (3) not already well-known as a Simpson's Paradox case. The aim is a genuine empirical test, not an illustration.
data.NextSuggestion: After witnessing the third study's plane coordinates, the model has three empirical points (kidney, Berkeley, new study) plus two structural controls (balanced-synthetic, kidney-balanced). Ask: do studies with IsConfounder=TRUE tend to have higher AllocationDistortion than studies with CausalRole='contested' or 'mediator'? This tests whether the allocation-based measure tracks causal structure.

data.Title: CausalStructureSignal: does AllocationDistortion track causal role?
data.NewConcept: CausalRoleGroup field on StratumVariables ('true-confounder'|'contested'|'mediator'|'collider'|'none'); AvgDistortionByRole aggregation on ModelSummary
data.DomainQuestion: Do true confounders (IsConfounder=TRUE) produce systematically higher AllocationDistortion than contested or mediator variables? If yes, AllocationDistortion implicitly encodes causal information even though it is computed purely from allocation counts — no causal graph required. That would be a non-obvious finding.
data.MockDataNote: Current data: kidney-1986 (true-confounder, AllocationDistortion=0.100), berkeley-1973 (contested, AllocationDistortion=0.193). Berkeley's distortion is higher despite its confounder being contested — which initially looks like a counter-example. But Berkeley's allocation bias (women disproportionately applied to selective departments) is structurally identical to a true confounder. The causal label 'contested' is a human judgment; the allocation bias is an arithmetic fact. The model reveals that the DAG doesn't need the causal label to detect the distortion.
data.NextSuggestion: If AllocationDistortion does not reliably track causal role (as the current two-study evidence suggests), the instrument is measuring something distinct from confounding — it is measuring allocation-induced signal distortion regardless of causal origin. Name this precisely: derive a DistortionType field that classifies studies by the geometric signature of their distortion, independent of the causal story.

data.Title: DistortionType: classify studies by the geometry of their distortion, independent of causal label
data.NewConcept: DistortionType field on TreatmentRankings: derived from (IsSignFlip, ReversalIntensity, AllocationDistortion magnitude) — a geometric classification independent of StratumVariable.CausalRole
data.DomainQuestion: Can the plane's geometry alone classify studies into meaningful types — without any causal story? Proposed types: (A) full-reversal: IsSignFlip=TRUE, ReversalIntensity=1.0 (kidney-1986); (B) partial-reversal: IsSignFlip=TRUE, ReversalIntensity<1.0 (berkeley-1973); (C) distortion-no-flip: IsSignFlip=FALSE but AllocationDistortion>0 (possible with near-threshold studies); (D) null: IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion≈0 (both controls). Does this four-type taxonomy cover all geometrically distinct cases?
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986 → type A. berkeley-1973 → type B. kidney-balanced and balanced-synthetic → type D. Type C (distortion without flip) does not yet appear in the data — it requires a study where allocation compresses but does not reverse the signal. The third real dataset (loop-21) may or may not populate this cell. The taxonomy predicts it exists; the next real dataset tests that prediction.
data.NextSuggestion: Pour a fourth real dataset specifically chosen to be a candidate type-C study — a study where there is known allocation imbalance but no published report of a reversal. If AllocationDistortion > 0 and IsSignFlip=FALSE, the instrument has identified a new, geometrically distinct class of misleading aggregate. That is a novel empirical finding.

data.Title: Type-C witness: a real study with allocation distortion but no sign flip
data.NewConcept: Sixth study chosen to be a candidate type-C; DistortionType=C if AllocationDistortion>0 and IsSignFlip=FALSE; empirical confirmation that type-C exists in the published literature
data.DomainQuestion: Does a study with measurable allocation distortion that does not cross the sign-flip threshold exist in published data? If yes, the taxonomy has four populated cells and the instrument is empirically complete across its own type space. If no study populates type-C, ask whether type-C is geometrically impossible or merely uncommon.
data.MockDataNote: To be filled after dataset selection. Candidate domain: a surgical outcomes study where senior surgeons handle harder cases (allocation bias present) but the pooled rate gap is in the same direction as the per-stratum gap (no flip) — just compressed. The DAG should witness AllocationDistortion > 0 (the allocation is doing work) but IsSignFlip=FALSE (not enough to reverse).
data.NextSuggestion: With all four types potentially witnessed, ask the highest-stakes question: which type causes the most harm in practice? Type-A (full reversal) is the most dramatic, but type-B (partial reversal excluded by the binary definition) may be more dangerous because it goes undetected. Derive PolicyImplication: what action does each DistortionType warrant from a researcher or policymaker?

data.Title: PolicyImplication: what does each DistortionType warrant a researcher to do?
data.NewConcept: PolicyImplication field on TreatmentRankings (derived from DistortionType and IsParadoxExplained): 'stratify-immediately', 'investigate-confounder', 'monitor-allocation', 'none'
data.DomainQuestion: Given a study's geometric classification (DistortionType) and causal explanation status (IsParadoxExplained), what is the correct epistemic action? This converts the plane's geometry into a decision — the first step toward making the instrument actionable for researchers who do not understand the underlying algebra.
data.MockDataNote: Type-A + explained (kidney-1986): stratify-immediately — the confounder is known, the reversal is confirmed, the correct answer is already derivable from the stratified data. Type-B + unexplained (berkeley-1973): investigate-confounder — the sign flip is real, the confounder is contested, further causal investigation is warranted before acting on the pooled result. Type-D + no distortion: none — the pooled analysis is trustworthy. The PolicyImplication field makes the plane's output directly actionable.
data.NextSuggestion: Test PolicyImplication against a real case where a wrong policy decision was made using a pooled analysis. Does the DAG's PolicyImplication for that study correctly flag it as 'stratify-immediately' or 'investigate-confounder' before the policy error was known? This is the retrospective validation test.

data.Title: Retrospective validation: does the instrument flag known historical policy errors?
data.NewConcept: Retrospective test study: a real case where a pooled analysis drove a policy decision later shown to be wrong due to confounding; PlaneCoordinateAtDecisionTime vs PlaneCoordinateWithFullData
data.DomainQuestion: Given only the data available at the time of the policy decision, does the instrument's PolicyImplication = 'stratify-immediately' or 'investigate-confounder'? If yes, the instrument would have flagged the error before the harm occurred. If no, what data would have been needed?
data.MockDataNote: To be filled after candidate selection. Known cases: HRT and cardiovascular disease (observational studies showed protection; RCTs showed harm — confounded by healthy-user bias); aspirin in children and Reye's syndrome; early AIDS drug trials stratified by disease stage. Each is a case where a pooled conclusion drove clinical guidelines later reversed. Pour the original data through the DAG and witness what the instrument would have said.
data.NextSuggestion: If the instrument successfully flags the retrospective case, the model has demonstrated retrospective validity. The next question is prospective reach: can the instrument be applied to currently published studies using only their published tables, before any error has been detected? This is the literature screening phase.

data.Title: Literature screen: apply the instrument to currently published studies using only published summary statistics
data.NewConcept: LiteratureCandidates rows in a new table: studies from current literature with ScreeningApprox coordinates computed from published tables; RawDataRequired boolean (whether exact coordinates need raw access)
data.DomainQuestion: Which currently published studies have high estimated AllocationDistortion from their published summary statistics alone? The screening approximation (loop-20) determines which studies can be classified without raw data. These are the candidates for the instrument's first prospective finding.
data.MockDataNote: To be filled after literature survey. Candidate domains based on known allocation-bias patterns: (1) cancer screening trials (high-risk patients get screened more — selection bias structurally identical to confounder); (2) socioeconomic stratification in educational outcome studies; (3) surgical volume-outcome studies (high-volume centres take harder cases). Priority: studies with large pooled sample sizes and published per-stratum subgroup tables.
data.NextSuggestion: Select the highest-scoring candidate and request or simulate raw stratum-level data to compute exact plane coordinates. Compare to the screening approximation. If the approximation correctly classifies the study (same DistortionType), the instrument is validated prospectively. If not, refine the approximation formula.

data.Title: Prospective validation: does a high-scoring literature candidate confirm its screening classification?
data.NewConcept: First prospective validation study: exact plane coordinates from raw data vs screening approximation; ProspectiveClassificationAccuracy on ModelSummary
data.DomainQuestion: The instrument's prospective value depends on the screening approximation being accurate enough to classify studies correctly without raw data. Does the highest-scoring literature candidate confirm or refute its screening classification? A confirmed type-B finding in a currently influential study would be the model's most significant empirical output.
data.MockDataNote: To be filled after dataset is obtained. The key test: does DistortionType from exact coordinates match DistortionType from screening approximation? If yes for the top candidate, repeat for two more candidates to establish a prospective accuracy rate.
data.NextSuggestion: With prospective validation complete (or partially complete), the instrument is ready for formal specification. Write the instrument definition as a set of computable rules derived from the DAG: the five fields (WeightedStratumGapSum, SignedPooledGap, IsSignFlip, AllocationDistortion, DistortionType), the four-type taxonomy, the PolicyImplication mapping, and the screening approximation formula. This is the publishable artefact.

data.Title: Instrument specification: the five fields, four types, and the screening formula
data.NewConcept: Formal instrument specification as a first-class InstrumentSpec table in the rulebook: one row per instrument component (field name, formula, interpretation, DAG node it traces to); machine-verifiable derivation chain
data.DomainQuestion: Can the full instrument be specified precisely enough that another researcher could apply it to a new dataset without understanding the DAG? The InstrumentSpec table IS the specification — not a document alongside the rulebook, but a first-class entity within it. Each row is a traceable claim.
data.MockDataNote: InstrumentSpec rows: (1) SignedPooledGap = PooledRateA − PooledRateB [traces to TreatmentRankings]; (2) WeightedStratumGapSum = Σ(StratumGap × StratumFraction) [traces to StratumSummaries]; (3) IsSignFlip = sign(WSGSUM) ≠ sign(SignedPooledGap) [traces to both]; (4) AllocationDistortion = |WSGSUM − SignedPooledGap| [derived]; (5) DistortionType = f(IsSignFlip, ReversalIntensity) [derived]. Five rows. Every claim machine-verifiable against the current seed data.
data.NextSuggestion: The instrument is specified. The final question is whether the binary IsReversal definition should be formally retired in favour of IsSignFlip. Derive IsReversal_v2 = IsSignFlip as a new calculated field on TreatmentRankings and compare it to IsReversal across all studies. Document which studies change classification and why IsReversal_v2 is the better definition.

data.Title: IsReversal_v2: retire the unanimity criterion — IsSignFlip is the correct definition
data.NewConcept: IsReversal_v2 = IsSignFlip on TreatmentRankings; DefinitionDelta = IsReversal_v2 XOR IsReversal (studies that change classification); formal statement of why IsSignFlip supersedes the unanimity criterion
data.DomainQuestion: IsReversal (old) requires the pooled loser to win in EVERY stratum. IsReversal_v2 requires only that the equal-weight pooled gap have the opposite sign to the actual pooled gap. Which definition is correct? Berkeley has DefinitionDelta=TRUE — it is FALSE under the old definition and TRUE under the new one. Does any study have DefinitionDelta=TRUE in the opposite direction (old=TRUE, new=FALSE)? If not, IsReversal_v2 strictly generalises IsReversal.
data.MockDataNote: Current four studies: kidney-1986: old=TRUE, new=TRUE, delta=FALSE. berkeley-1973: old=FALSE, new=TRUE, delta=TRUE. kidney-balanced: old=FALSE, new=FALSE, delta=FALSE. balanced-synthetic: old=FALSE, new=FALSE, delta=FALSE. IsReversal_v2 strictly includes IsReversal and adds Berkeley. No study goes from TRUE to FALSE. The new definition is a strict generalisation, not a replacement that reclassifies existing positive cases. This is the cleanest possible definitional upgrade.
data.NextSuggestion: The model now has a publishable finding: IsReversal_v2 strictly generalises the binary unanimity definition, Berkeley 1973 is correctly classified as a Simpson's Paradox under it, and the AllocationDistortion measure ranks Berkeley as more paradoxical than kidney-1986. Write the preprint abstract from these three claims and the four DAG-derived witnessed coordinates.

Studies.Description: Table: Studies — a clinical or empirical study comparing two or more treatments for a condition, with patient cases stratified by a confounding variable. The top-level entity in the domain.

schema.name: StudyId
schema.Description: Unique slug for this study (e.g. kidney-1986, berkeley-1973).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors StudyId.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Human-readable title of the study.

schema.name: Source
schema.Description: Citation or description of the original data source.

schema.name: SourceUrl
schema.Description: URL to the original source.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total number of cases (trials) across all CaseCells in this study.

schema.name: CellCount
schema.Description: Number of CaseCell rows for this study.

data.Title: Kidney Stone Treatment Study (Charig et al. 1986)
data.Source: Charig CR et al. Comparison of treatment of renal calculi by open surgery, percutaneous nephrolithotomy, and extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy. Br Med J. 1986;292:879.

data.Title: Balanced Synthetic Study (degenerate case — reversal structurally impossible)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset constructed to demonstrate that perfectly balanced treatment allocation across strata algebraically prevents Simpson's Paradox. Not based on real data.

data.Title: Kidney Stone Counterfactual (balanced allocation — reversal algebraically impossible)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset derived from Charig 1986 kidney stone rates but with equal treatment allocation across strata (175 cases per cell). Demonstrates that the reversal in the original study is purely an allocation artifact.

data.Title: UC Berkeley Graduate Admissions (Bickel et al. 1975) — contested confounder
data.Source: Bickel PJ, Hammel EA, O'Connell JW. Sex Bias in Graduate Admissions: Data from Berkeley. Science. 1975;187:398-404.

Treatments.Description: Table: Treatments — the interventions being compared within a study. Each treatment belongs to one study.

schema.name: TreatmentId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-A, kidney-1986-B).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: Short label used in CaseCells and comparisons (e.g. A, B).

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Plain-language description of what this treatment is.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for this treatment across all strata.

schema.name: TotalSuccesses
schema.Description: Total successes for this treatment across all strata.

schema.name: PooledSuccessRate
schema.Description: Pooled (across all strata) success rate for this treatment: TotalSuccesses / TotalCases.

data.Description: Open surgery / percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) — the more invasive procedure, used preferentially for large stones.
Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) — the less invasive procedure, used more for small stones.
Open surgery / PCNL — same per-stratum rates as kidney-1986 (small:93.1%, large:73.0%) but now allocated equally (50%) to each stratum. Counterfactual treatment A.
ESWL — same per-stratum rates as kidney-1986 (small:86.7%, large:68.8%) but allocated equally (50%) to each stratum. Counterfactual treatment B.
Synthetic treatment A — 60% success rate in both strata. Allocated equally (50%) to each stratum.
Synthetic treatment B — 50% success rate in both strata. Allocated equally (50%) to each stratum.
Male applicants (labeled A). Pooled admission rate appears higher than female.
Female applicants (labeled B). Pooled admission rate appears lower, but per-department rates are equal or higher in most departments.

Strata.Description: Table: Strata — the subdivisions of patients within a study by the value of the confounding variable (e.g. stone size). Each stratum belongs to one study.

schema.name: StratumId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small, kidney-1986-large).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: Short label used in CaseCells (e.g. small, large, dept-A).

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Plain-language description of what defines this stratum.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this stratum across all treatments.

data.Description: Patients with small kidney stones — easier to treat, higher baseline success rate regardless of treatment.
Patients with large kidney stones — harder to treat, lower baseline success rate regardless of treatment. Preferentially assigned to Treatment A.
Small kidney stones — same baseline rates as kidney-1986 small stratum but now receiving exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Counterfactual stratum.
Large kidney stones — same baseline rates as kidney-1986 large stratum but now receiving exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Equal allocation prevents confounding.
Easy stratum — higher baseline success rate. Receives exactly 50% of each treatment's cases.
Hard stratum — lower baseline success rate. Receives exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Equal allocation prevents confounding.
Dept A — highly competitive (low admission rate ~64%). Women applied here at roughly equal rates to men.
Dept B — competitive (~35% admission). Women applied here less often.
Dept C — moderately selective (~34% admission). More balanced gender application.
Dept D — open (~64% admission). Women applied here most heavily — this is the easy department that women disproportionately chose.

CaseCells.Description: Table: CaseCells — the raw leaves of the DAG. One row per (study, stratum, treatment) combination recording the aggregate count of successes and total cases. Every derived fact in the model traces back to these numbers. Nothing above this row is a raw input.

schema.name: CaseCellId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small-A).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: The stratum this cell belongs to (matches Strata.StratumLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: The treatment applied in this cell (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: Successes
schema.Description: Number of patients in this cell who had a successful outcome. RAW INPUT — never derived.

schema.name: Cases
schema.Description: Total number of patients in this cell. RAW INPUT — never derived.

schema.name: CellSuccessRate
schema.Description: Success rate within this cell: Successes / Cases. The first derived fact in the DAG.

schema.name: TotalCasesForTreatment
schema.Description: Total cases across ALL strata for this treatment in this study — the denominator for TreatmentExposureFraction.

schema.name: TreatmentExposureFraction
schema.Description: Fraction of this treatment's total cases that fall in this stratum: Cases / TotalCasesForTreatment. High imbalance across strata is the mechanism of confounding — when treatments are allocated very differently across strata, the pooled rate conflates treatment effect with stratum difficulty.

StratumSummaries.Description: Table: StratumSummaries — one row per (study, stratum, treatment). Computes the success rate WITHIN each stratum for each treatment. This is the stratified view that contradicts the pooled conclusion when a Simpson's Paradox reversal is present. Each row belongs to both a Stratum and a Treatment in the same Study.

schema.name: StratumSummaryId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small-A).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: Which stratum (matches Strata.StratumLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: Which treatment (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: StratumSuccesses
schema.Description: Total successes in this (stratum, treatment) cell for this study.

schema.name: StratumCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this (stratum, treatment) cell for this study.

schema.name: StratumSuccessRate
schema.Description: Success rate within this stratum for this treatment: StratumSuccesses / StratumCases. The true per-stratum picture.

schema.name: StratumSuccessesA
schema.Description: Successes for treatment A in this (study, stratum) — used to determine the per-stratum winner.

schema.name: StratumCasesA
schema.Description: Cases for treatment A in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumRateA
schema.Description: Success rate for treatment A in this stratum.

schema.name: StratumSuccessesB
schema.Description: Successes for treatment B in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumCasesB
schema.Description: Cases for treatment B in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumRateB
schema.Description: Success rate for treatment B in this stratum.

schema.name: StratumWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins in this stratum: 'A' if StratumRateA > StratumRateB, else 'B'. Used by TreatmentRankings to detect paradox.

schema.name: StudyTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases across ALL strata and ALL treatments in this study. The denominator for StratumFraction.

schema.name: StratumTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this stratum across both treatments (A + B combined). Numerator for StratumFraction.

schema.name: StratumFraction
schema.Description: Fraction of the study's total cases that fall in this stratum: StratumTotalCases / StudyTotalCases. The weight the confounder assigns to each stratum. A large StratumFraction on a low-success stratum pulls the pooled rate down; when that stratum is also over-allocated to one treatment, it creates the reversal.

schema.name: WeightedStratumRate
schema.Description: StratumFraction × StratumSuccessRate. The contribution of this (stratum, treatment) row to the study-level pooled rate. Summing WeightedStratumRate across all strata for a given treatment reconstructs that treatment's pooled rate from stratum weights — witnessing the mechanism equation.

schema.name: TreatmentACasesHere
schema.Description: Cases for treatment A in this stratum — used to compute TreatmentA's allocation fraction in this stratum.

schema.name: TreatmentBCasesHere
schema.Description: Cases for treatment B in this stratum — used to compute TreatmentB's allocation fraction in this stratum.

schema.name: TreatmentATotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for treatment A across all strata in this study — denominator for AllocationFractionA.

schema.name: TreatmentBTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for treatment B across all strata in this study — denominator for AllocationFractionB.

schema.name: AllocationFractionA
schema.Description: Fraction of treatment A's total cases that land in this stratum: TreatmentACasesHere / TreatmentATotalCases. Equivalent to TreatmentExposureFraction for A.

schema.name: AllocationFractionB
schema.Description: Fraction of treatment B's total cases that land in this stratum: TreatmentBCasesHere / TreatmentBTotalCases.

schema.name: AllocationBias
schema.Description: AllocationFractionA minus AllocationFractionB: how much more of treatment A (relative to B) is concentrated in this stratum. Positive = A is over-allocated here; negative = B is over-allocated here. When AllocationBias is large and negative in a low-success stratum, treatment A is under-represented in the hard cases — making A look worse in the pooled view than it is in any stratum. This is the confounder's fingerprint as a signed number.

schema.name: StratumGap
schema.Description: Signed rate difference within this stratum: StratumRateA minus StratumRateB. Positive = A wins here; negative = B wins here; zero = tied. When every stratum has StratumGap > 0 but the pooled gap is negative, the contradiction IS Simpson's Paradox made arithmetically visible in one column. Multiply StratumGap × StratumFraction and sum across strata to get the equal-weight pooled gap (the signal allocation would produce without confounding).

schema.name: WeightedStratumGap
schema.Description: StratumGap × StratumFraction: the contribution of this stratum's treatment-rate difference to the equal-weight pooled gap. Summing WeightedStratumGap across all TreatmentA rows for a study gives WeightedStratumGapSum — the pooled gap the study would show under balanced allocation. This is the per-stratum building block of the sign-flip test.

ModelSummary.Description: Table: ModelSummary — one row describing the epistemic coverage of this entire model instance. Aggregates across all TreatmentRankings to witness how many studies were examined, how many showed a full reversal, how many were explained, and the average paradox strength. The model holds a mirror to itself.

schema.name: ModelSummaryId
schema.Description: Unique identifier for this model instance.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: ReversalCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal=TRUE (strict full reversal).

schema.name: NonReversalCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal=FALSE.

schema.name: StudyCount
schema.Description: Total number of TreatmentRankings in this model: ReversalCount + NonReversalCount.

schema.name: ExplainedCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsParadoxExplained=TRUE (reversal AND confirmed confounder).

schema.name: ZeroStrengthCount
schema.Description: Studies with StrataWonByLoser=0: no strata go against the pooled winner. These are structurally paradox-free.

schema.name: PartialCount
schema.Description: Studies with ParadoxStrength > 0 but IsReversal=FALSE: partial paradoxes real but not formally complete.

schema.name: TotalParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Sum of ParadoxStrength across all studies. Used to compute average.

schema.name: AvgParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Average ParadoxStrength across all studies: TotalParadoxStrength / StudyCount. A scalar summary of how paradox-rich this dataset is.

StratumVariables.Description: Table: StratumVariables — one row per study, naming the variable that defines the strata and describing its causal role. A confounder affects both treatment assignment AND outcome; a mediator is downstream of treatment; a contested variable is one where the causal role is disputed. IsConfounder is derived from these two boolean flags.

schema.name: StratumVariableId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-stone-size).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: VariableName
schema.Description: The name of the stratifying variable (e.g. stone_size, department, age_group).

schema.name: CausalRole
schema.Description: The variable's causal role: 'confounder' (affects both assignment and outcome), 'mediator' (downstream of treatment), 'contested' (causal role disputed), 'unknown'.

schema.name: AffectsTreatmentAssignment
schema.Description: TRUE if this variable causally influences which treatment a patient receives. Required for confounding.

schema.name: AffectsOutcome
schema.Description: TRUE if this variable causally influences the outcome independently of treatment. Required for confounding.

schema.name: IsConfounder
schema.Description: TRUE when AffectsTreatmentAssignment AND AffectsOutcome AND CausalRole = 'confounder'. A confounder is the classic driver of Simpson's Paradox.

schema.name: MechanismNote
schema.Description: Plain-language description of how this variable creates the confounding: why it affects both assignment and outcome.

data.MechanismNote: Large kidney stones were preferentially assigned to Treatment A (the more aggressive procedure), while small stones more often received Treatment B. Independently, large stones have lower baseline cure rates regardless of treatment. This dual influence — on both assignment and on outcome — is what drives the pooled reversal.

Department choice correlates with both gender (women applied disproportionately to competitive departments) and admission rate (competitive departments admit fewer applicants regardless of gender). However, department choice may itself be downstream of gender — if women systematically self-select into harder departments due to societal factors, then department is a mediator, not a confounder. The causal role is genuinely contested in the literature. IsConfounder=FALSE because CausalRole='contested', not because the flags are false.

In this counterfactual, stone size still affects outcome (hard cases have lower success rates) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — both treatments receive exactly 50% of their cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is now independent of stone size, no confounding occurs and AllocationBias=0 everywhere. IsConfounder=FALSE (AffectsTreatmentAssignment=false). The reversal is impossible by construction.

The stratum variable affects outcome (easy vs hard conditions) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — each treatment receives exactly 50% of its cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is independent of stratum, no confounding can occur and the pooled rate must agree with every per-stratum comparison.

TreatmentRankings.Description: Table: TreatmentRankings — one row per treatment-pair comparison within a study. Derives the pooled winner, the per-stratum winner, and the IsReversal boolean. The paradox emerges here as a derived fact: IsReversal is true when the pooled winner and the per-stratum winner disagree.

schema.name: TreatmentRankingId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-A-vs-B).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: TreatmentA
schema.Description: Label for the first treatment in the comparison (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentB
schema.Description: Label for the second treatment in the comparison.

schema.name: TotalCasesA
schema.Description: Total cases for TreatmentA across all strata in this study.

schema.name: TotalSuccessesA
schema.Description: Total successes for TreatmentA across all strata.

schema.name: PooledRateA
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for TreatmentA: TotalSuccessesA / TotalCasesA.

schema.name: TotalCasesB
schema.Description: Total cases for TreatmentB across all strata.

schema.name: TotalSuccessesB
schema.Description: Total successes for TreatmentB across all strata.

schema.name: PooledRateB
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for TreatmentB: TotalSuccessesB / TotalCasesB.

schema.name: PooledWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins when strata are ignored: the one with the higher pooled rate.

schema.name: StratumCount
schema.Description: Number of strata in this study. Counted from StratumSummaries filtered to TreatmentA rows (one row per stratum).

schema.name: StrataWonByA
schema.Description: Number of strata where A wins: count StratumSummaries rows whose TreatmentLabel=TreatmentA AND StratumWinner=TreatmentA. These are the one-per-stratum sentinel rows.

schema.name: StrataWonByB
schema.Description: Number of strata where B wins: count StratumSummaries rows whose TreatmentLabel=TreatmentA AND StratumWinner=TreatmentB.

schema.name: PerStratumWinner
schema.Description: The treatment that wins in every stratum — 'A' if StrataWonByA = StratumCount, 'B' if StrataWonByB = StratumCount, else 'none' (no unanimous per-stratum winner).

schema.name: IsReversal
schema.Description: TRUE when the pooled winner and the per-stratum winner disagree — i.e. Simpson's Paradox is present. FALSE otherwise. This is the paradox as a derived fact, not a modeled entity.

schema.name: ConfoundersInStudy
schema.Description: Count of StratumVariables in this study whose IsConfounder = TRUE. When > 0, the paradox has a causal explanation.

schema.name: IsParadoxExplained
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal is present AND at least one confirmed confounder exists in the study. The model witnesses its own explanatory completeness — or its limits.

schema.name: PooledGap
schema.Description: Absolute difference between the two pooled rates: |PooledRateA - PooledRateB|. The size of the aggregate misleading signal.

schema.name: StrataWonByLoser
schema.Description: Number of strata won by the pooled loser — the counter-signal. For full reversals this equals StratumCount; for partial paradoxes it is between 0 and StratumCount.

schema.name: ParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Scalar severity of the paradox: PooledGap × (StrataWonByLoser / StratumCount). Zero when no strata go against the pooled winner. Positive for partial paradoxes. Maximum when every stratum contradicts the pooled result.

schema.name: PooledRateFromWeightsA
schema.Description: Pooled rate for TreatmentA reconstructed as a weighted average of stratum rates: SUM(WeightedStratumRate) across all stratum rows for TreatmentA. Must match PooledRateA — this witnesses the mechanism equation: the pooled rate IS a weighted average of stratum rates, weighted by StratumFraction.

schema.name: PooledRateFromWeightsB
schema.Description: Pooled rate for TreatmentB reconstructed as a weighted average. Must match PooledRateB. If it does, the mechanism equation is verified: reversal is purely a consequence of differential weighting.

schema.name: ReversalIntensity
schema.Description: StrataWonByLoser / StratumCount: the fraction of strata that go against the pooled winner. Zero for no paradox, 1.0 for full reversal, between 0 and 1 for partial. This is the allocation-side measure of how deeply the confounding has penetrated the stratified view.

schema.name: ThresholdMargin
schema.Description: ReversalIntensity minus 0.5: positive when more than half the strata go against the pooled winner (reversal is robust), zero at the tipping point (exactly half), negative when fewer than half oppose the pooled winner (reversal is fragile or absent). A study with ThresholdMargin > 0 is robustly paradoxical; one with ThresholdMargin < 0 has a weak or absent paradox.

schema.name: SignedPooledGap
schema.Description: Signed difference between pooled rates: PooledRateA minus PooledRateB. Positive = A wins pooled; negative = B wins pooled. Unlike PooledGap (which is absolute), this preserves direction — essential for detecting sign flips between the equal-weight and actual pooled signals.

schema.name: WeightedStratumGapSum
schema.Description: Sum of WeightedStratumGap (= StratumGap × StratumFraction) across all StratumSummaries rows where TreatmentLabel = TreatmentA for this study. This is the equal-weight pooled gap — the gap the pooled analysis would produce if allocation were perfectly balanced across strata. When this has the opposite sign to SignedPooledGap, the allocation has not merely compressed the signal: it has flipped it entirely.

schema.name: IsSignFlip
schema.Description: TRUE when WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs — the allocation has reversed the direction of the pooled signal relative to what equal-weight strata would show. This is the continuous generalisation of IsReversal: it does not require every stratum to agree, only that the equal-weight estimate and the actual pooled estimate point in opposite directions. Berkeley 1973 satisfies IsSignFlip=TRUE despite IsReversal=FALSE.

_meta._CMCC_Summary: Domain mirror of Simpson's Paradox. Entities: Studies, Treatments, Strata, CaseCells. CellSuccessRate is the first derived fact. Pooled rates, per-stratum rates, and the reversal fall out as higher-order derived nodes in subsequent loops.
